There is an uncomfortable question that software developers should probably stop avoiding.
If AI can write the code, what exactly is software experience worth?
Not in the abstract. Not in a nostalgic panel discussion about craftsmanship. Not in a corporate memo where everyone agrees that “human judgment remains essential” because everyone in the room needs that sentence to be true.
I mean the practical version.
A client has a problem. A company has a workflow. A small business needs an internal tool. Someone needs a web app, or a desktop app, or a device, or a dashboard, or a script, or an API. AI can now generate a frightening amount of the implementation. It can scaffold the project, write the functions, connect the database, make the UI, generate the tests, fix the bugs, and explain the deployment.
So why pay an experienced software developer?
One answer is: “Because someone has to inspect the code.”
That is not entirely false, but it is also not enough. It sounds like a retreat line. It sounds like the person who used to build the machine is now asking to be paid to stand next to the machine and nod solemnly while it runs.
Another answer is: “Because architecture matters.”
That is also true, but too vague. Architecture is one of those words that can mean everything or nothing, depending on who is defending their invoice.
Another answer is: “Because experience matters.”
Yes. Maybe. But that is exactly the claim under examination.
The uncomfortable possibility is that much of what developers used to call experience was actually stored implementation knowledge. We knew which library to use, which syntax worked, which API call had the odd parameter, which framework had the better documentation, how to structure the build, how to debug the runtime, how to get from blank editor to working software.
AI is rapidly eating that territory.
It is not eating it perfectly. It hallucinates. It makes poor choices. It writes plausible nonsense. It will confidently generate the wrong thing. But the direction of travel is obvious. The raw ability to produce code is becoming less rare. The old monopoly over implementation is breaking.
So what remains?
Maybe less than we want.
But not nothing.
The Vibe Coder Is Not Necessarily Stupid
It would be comforting to draw a clean line between the experienced AI-assisted developer and the “vibe coder.”
The vibe coder, in this caricature, merely types a wish into a chatbot and celebrates whatever comes out. He does not understand the code. He does not test it. He does not know where the data goes. He does not know what will happen when the demo becomes a business process. He has mistaken “it runs on my laptop” for software development.
There is truth in that caricature. But it is too easy.
A clever vibe coder can also ask AI for options. He can ask whether a project should be a web app, a desktop app, a mobile app, a local tool, or an embedded device. He can ask for a comparison between Electron and Tauri, React and Svelte, Node and Bun, Arduino and ESP-IDF, PostgreSQL and SQLite. He can ask for tradeoffs, failure modes, deployment implications, maintenance risks, and security concerns.
The AI will answer. Often quite well.
So the experienced developer cannot simply say, “I know things the vibe coder does not.”
That claim is becoming weaker every month.
The distinction must be subtler.
The vibe coder can obtain advice.
The experienced developer can distrust advice at the right places.
That may not sound heroic. It is certainly not the triumphant defense of software experience that developers might prefer. But it is probably closer to the truth.
The First Decision Is What Kind of Thing This Is
A client often says: “I need an app.”
That phrase is almost useless.
An app could mean a public web application, a private dashboard, a desktop utility, a mobile app, a command-line tool, a browser extension, a local server, a kiosk interface, a spreadsheet with automation, a content management workflow, an API integration, or a small physical device with buttons and a screen.
Before AI writes the code, someone has to decide what kind of thing should exist.
This first decision is already valuable. Some problems should be web-based. Some should be local. Some should run on a phone. Some should be a cheap embedded device. Some should not be custom software at all.
But the more interesting decisions come after that.
Suppose we decide it is a desktop app. Should it be Electron, Tauri, Qt, Flutter, a local web server with a browser frontend, or even a text-based interface? Suppose we decide it is a web app. Should it be React with Node, React with Bun, a boring server-rendered app, a static site with a small API, WebAssembly, or something else entirely? Suppose we decide it is device-based. Should it use Arduino, FreeRTOS, the vendor SDK, MicroPython, PlatformIO, ESP-IDF, M5Stack libraries, or a custom lower-level approach?
AI can generate code for all of these.
That is precisely why the choice matters more.
When only a few stacks were available to a developer, the technology choice was partly determined by what the developer already knew. That was limiting, but it also reduced the decision space. Now the decision space is much wider. AI can make many more choices look plausible.
And that creates a new danger.
AI does not merely accelerate good software.
It accelerates premature commitment.
A generated prototype can become impressive very quickly. The screen appears. The buttons work. The API responds. The device lights up. The demo succeeds. Everyone relaxes.
But what exactly has been validated?
Not necessarily the architecture. Not the deployment model. Not the update path. Not the operational burden. Not the maintenance story. Not the client’s ability to live with the thing after the initial thrill is gone.
Often, the demo has only validated that AI can produce a surface.
The surface is not the software.
Technologies Are Chosen by Their Acceptable Failures
Here is the central point:
Technologies are not chosen by their strengths. They are chosen by their acceptable failures.
This sounds backwards at first. We are used to comparing technologies by their virtues. This one is fast. That one is lightweight. This one has a rich ecosystem. That one has native performance. This one has a beautiful developer experience. That one is modern. This one is simple. That one is powerful.
But in a real project, the strengths are often the advertisement.
The failures are the bill.
Electron is heavy, but its failure mode may be acceptable: a large application, familiar web technologies, a stable cross-platform runtime, and a large ecosystem. That can be a perfectly reasonable bargain.
Tauri is elegant and lightweight, but its failure mode may involve Rust boundaries, webview differences, packaging details, and a smaller pool of maintainers. That may also be a perfectly reasonable bargain. Or it may not.
A web app is universal until the client needs offline operation, local files, hardware access, kiosk behavior, or predictable performance on a weak machine.
A mobile app is attractive until the real project becomes app-store accounts, approvals, updates, device compatibility, push notifications, permissions, and support.
Arduino is fast and friendly until the project needs reliable Bluetooth behavior, careful memory management, low-power operation, timing guarantees, production diagnostics, or deeper control of the device.
A fashionable runtime may be delightful until the client needs boring hosting, conservative maintenance, and another contractor who can take over in two years.
A text-based interface may look primitive until the actual user is an internal operator who values speed, clarity, keyboard control, and reliability more than visual polish.
The professional question is not “Which technology is best?”
There is no best.
The professional question is:
Which failure can this client afford?
That is not a coding question. It is not even purely a technical question. It is a judgment question.
Experience as Scar Tissue
This is where experience still matters, if it matters at all.
Not as prestige. Not as priesthood. Not as the right to say “I have been doing this for thirty years” and expect applause.
Experience matters as scar tissue.
That phrase hurts a little, especially if you are the person whose experience is being described. It reduces years of learning to a history of damage. But software developers have always understood this, even when we used more flattering words.
Why are experienced developers wary of fads?
Scar tissue.
Why do they mistrust rewrites?
Scar tissue.
Why do they ask about deployment before the demo?
Scar tissue.
Why do they care about backups, logs, boring dependencies, boring databases, boring authentication, boring update paths?
Scar tissue.
They have seen the beautiful choice become the annoying reality. They have seen the quick prototype become the production system. They have seen the internal script become the finance department’s workflow. They have seen the elegant architecture become unmaintainable because only one person understood it. They have seen the library that saved a week cost three months. They have seen the device that worked on the bench fail in the field because power, cables, enclosures, provisioning, and recovery were treated as details.
The experienced developer is not necessarily smarter than the vibe coder.
He may simply have been wrong more often, in more expensive ways.
That is not a grand claim. But it is a real one.
Experience in the AI-gen era is not mostly “I know how to write this code.” It is more like:
“I have been fooled by this shape of success before.”
The New Developer Is a Chooser of Constraints
If AI writes the code, the human role moves upward. But it does not float away into vague “strategy.” It becomes more concrete, not less.
The human chooses constraints.
Use this runtime, not that one.
Make it local-first.
Avoid app stores.
Use SQLite.
Do not invent a user system.
Make deployment a folder copy.
Keep the hardware replaceable.
Use boring libraries.
Accept a heavier app to avoid a fragile build.
Accept a rougher interface to avoid a month of polish.
Make the prototype disposable.
Do not let the prototype become the product.
Write the tests around behavior, not implementation.
Document the assumptions.
Export the data.
Own the domain.
Back up the database.
Make failure visible.
Make recovery possible.
These are not glamorous decisions. They are also not obsolete decisions.
In fact, AI makes them more important because AI can fill in the implementation so quickly that bad constraints become working software before anyone has had time to object.
The vibe coder asks AI to build the thing.
The professional first decides what kind of thing should exist.
And then he decides what kind of thing should absolutely not exist.
The Corporate Worker and the Contractor
There are two versions of this problem.
The first is the corporate worker: the person developing and maintaining software inside a company.
Let us ignore, for the moment, legacy brownfield systems full of old human-written code. Those are important, but they are also transitional artifacts. The cleaner question is what value the developer adds in new AI-generated software inside an organization.
In that context, experience is valuable when it protects the company from internal chaos.
The corporate AI-gen developer should ask:
Does this fit the company’s infrastructure?
Can IT deploy it?
Can security approve it?
Can another team maintain it?
Does it integrate with identity, logging, databases, backups, monitoring, and existing workflows?
Will this become shadow IT?
Who owns it?
Who is paged when it breaks?
What happens when the employee who prompted it leaves?
The value is not that the developer personally typed the code. The value is that he prevents the organization from filling itse
